title: Leverage
summary: Leverage shows how strongly earnings react when sales, fixed costs, or financial commitments change.

topics.title: Operating leverage
topics.summary: Explains how fixed costs make EBIT react more strongly than sales.

sections.title: Degree of operating leverage
sections.body: DOL compares total contribution margin with EBIT. It is useful when you want to measure how sensitive operations are before interest and taxes.
bullets: The higher the weight of fixed costs, the more EBIT is amplified when sales change.
DOL does not measure profitability by itself. It measures sensitivity.

sections.title: What each part means
sections.body: The DOL logic becomes easier when you first separate revenue, variable costs, and fixed costs.
bullets: Total contribution margin shows how much remains to cover fixed costs and create EBIT.
Once fixed costs are covered, each extra sale pushes EBIT faster.
If EBIT is close to zero, DOL spikes and the company becomes more fragile.

exampleTable.title: Quick reading of the operating structure

cells: Each extra unit helps more with fixed costs

exampleTable.footnote: That is why operating leverage is usually higher in businesses with heavy plant, logistics, or fixed infrastructure.

sections.title: Mini guided case
sections.body: Assume you sell 1,200 units at 30 each, with a variable cost of 18 per unit and fixed costs of 9,000.
bullets: First compute total contribution margin: 1,200(30-18) = 14,400.
Then compute EBIT: 14,400 - 9,000 = 5,400.
Finally compute DOL: 14,400 / 5,400 = 2.67.
That means a 1% change in sales would move EBIT by roughly 2.67%.

sections.title: Common mistakes
sections.body: These mistakes usually appear when students first study DOL.
bullets: Confusing unit variable cost with total variable cost.
Entering sales revenue in Q when the formula expects units.
Interpreting DOL when EBIT is zero or negative.
Assuming that a high DOL is always good, when it also means higher risk.

topics.title: Financial leverage
topics.summary: Describes how debt, interest, and preferred dividends amplify earnings available to common shareholders.

sections.title: Degree of financial leverage
sections.body: DFL compares EBIT with the base that remains available to common shareholders after financial commitments are covered.
bullets: Interest and preferred dividends do not move directly with sales, so they make common-shareholder earnings more sensitive.
The smaller the base available to common shareholders, the higher DFL becomes.

formulaLegend.meaning: tax rate expressed as a decimal

sections.title: What DFL tells you
sections.body: DFL does not focus on sales. It focuses on how earnings available to common shareholders react when EBIT changes.
bullets: If DFL = 2.5, a 1% change in EBIT causes roughly a 2.5% change in earnings available to common shareholders.
Financial risk rises when interest and preferred dividends consume a large share of EBIT.
That is why a debt-heavy structure can raise expected return but also raise fragility.

exampleTable.title: Quick reading of DFL
exampleTable.footnote: A high DFL can help in favorable scenarios, but it also increases the penalty when EBIT drops.

sections.title: Mini guided case
sections.body: Assume EBIT is 10,000, interest is 2,000, preferred dividends are 2,400, and the tax rate is 40%.
bullets: First adjust preferred dividends for taxes: 2,400 × (1 / 0.60) = 4,000.
Then compute the base available to common shareholders: 10,000 - 2,000 - 4,000 = 4,000.
Finally compute DFL: 10,000 / 4,000 = 2.5.
That means earnings available to common shareholders move 2.5 times faster than EBIT.

sections.title: Common mistakes
sections.body: These issues often appear in classroom exercises.
bullets: Using net income instead of EBIT.
Forgetting to adjust preferred dividends for taxes.
Entering the tax rate as 0.40 when the field expects 40.
Accepting any result without checking whether the common-shareholder base remains positive.

topics.title: Combined leverage
topics.summary: Combines operations and financing to show how much earnings per share may change when sales change.

sections.title: Degree of combined leverage
sections.body: DCL joins the operating and financial effects. Use it when you want to connect sales with earnings per share or earnings available to common shareholders.
bullets: DCL summarizes two different decisions: cost structure and financing structure.
If both degrees are high, total sensitivity multiplies.

formulaLegend.meaning: earnings per share or common-shareholder earnings per share

sections.title: How to interpret it
sections.body: DCL tells you how much shareholder earnings change when sales change by 1%.
bullets: If DCL = 4, a 1% change in sales can cause roughly a 4% change in earnings per share.
This does not mean the firm always earns more. It means the final result becomes more sensitive.
A high DCL may be attractive in expansion, but also riskier in slowdown.

exampleTable.title: Example of the combined effect
exampleTable.footnote: That DCL suggests that a 5% sales change could move earnings per share by about 19.8%.

sections.title: When it is useful
sections.body: DCL is useful for strategy, not just for solving exercises.
bullets: Compare firms with different cost structures and debt levels.
Evaluate whether an increase in total risk is still reasonable for the business.
Understand why an asset-heavy and highly levered firm may have very volatile outcomes.

calculator.title: Leverage calculator
calculator.summary: Evaluate operating or financial sensitivity with a guided flow that changes by leverage type.
calculator.note: The degree tells you how many times a percentage change is amplified in the model's base outcome.

modes.label: Operating
modes.summary: Starts from sales, contribution margin, and fixed costs to estimate EBIT sensitivity.

modes.label: Financial
modes.summary: Starts from EBIT, interest, preferred dividends, and taxes to measure financial sensitivity.

fields.label: Q Units sold
fields.helperText: Number of units you expect to sell during the period.

fields.label: VC Variable cost per unit
fields.helperText: Cost that changes directly with each unit sold.

fields.label: FC Total fixed costs
fields.helperText: Costs that do not change with volume inside the relevant range.

fields.label: I Interest expense
fields.helperText: Interest payments committed for the period.

fields.label: PD Preferred dividends
fields.helperText: Dividends that must be paid to preferred shareholders.

fields.label: T Tax rate
fields.helperText: Enter the tax rate as a percentage. The formula will convert it to decimal form.
